user_prompt: you know how we are using `USE_BLUEDROID_MSBC`? add a switch for `USE_LIBSBC_MSBC`, and if neither are used, do the "assume it's PCM" way
agent_reply: Default is now **PCM callback mode** because both switches are commented in [conf.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:12):
- neither defined: assume HFP callbacks are already PCM and queue/dequeue PCM directly.
I also added [lib/sbc/library.json](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/sbc/library.json:1), plus the PlatformIO include/dependency entries so `USE_LIBSBC_MSBC` actually builds.
- default PCM mode: `pio run -e theflybringuptest` succeeds
The PCM default should be the one to flash for your next voicemail test.

user_prompt: was that the right move? if one of `USE_BLUEDROID_MSBC` or `USE_LIBSBC_MSBC`, can you still check for a header and see if PCM is the right move? or is that not possible? Do not change the file if it is not possible
agent_reply: Yes, that was the right move. Your logs strongly said “PCM is the real payload” despite the HFP audio state saying mSBC: 240-byte callbacks line up with 120 samples of 16-bit mono PCM, while mSBC encoded frames would look like framed 57-byte-ish data with recognizable headers.

I made that runtime-detectable in [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:183). If either `USE_BLUEDROID_MSBC` or `USE_LIBSBC_MSBC` is enabled, it now checks for an mSBC H2/header pattern before decoding. If the callback is PCM-sized and has no mSBC header, it logs that decision and queues it as PCM instead. That is possible because the header would be in the callback byte stream if ESP-IDF were handing us encoded mSBC frames.
I restored [conf.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:12) to the current default with both codec switches commented out.
Next run, the useful line to look for is `HFP callback payload detected as PCM...` or `encoded mSBC...`; that will tell us which branch it chose on the device.

commit_message: cleaned up diagnostics a bit, added mutex for pump functions

user_prompt: We have made a lot of progress, a lot of things are working the way I want
Walk through the documentation I wrote earlier, we might've deviated a lot. Warn me where the implementation does not match the written documentation, and tell me suggested changes
Note: I am thinking about making the device way more secure, and the `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` will eventually go away, so ignore things that involve that
agent_reply: 1. **The documented file-transfer/filesystem design no longer matches the code.**

The docs still say Wi-Fi provides an FTP server, the app treats onboard flash and microSD as separate disks, and LittleFS is used: [README.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/README.md:7), [file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/file-system-and-ftp-server-design.md:1).
- no FTP implementation beyond a readme in [lib/FtpServer/readme.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FtpServer/readme.md:1)
- a microSD-only HTTP file server in [WebServer.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WebServer.cpp:376)
- that HTTP server is only visibly initialized by the webserver test, not normal firmware startup: [test_webserver.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_webserver.cpp:53)
Suggested change: decide the real transfer direction now.
- If HTTP/web UI is the intended path, update README and filesystem docs from “FTP + LittleFS + two disks” to “microSD file access over HTTP/web UI” and delete or mark FTP notes as obsolete.
- If FTP and onboard flash still matter, they are implementation gaps, not documentation details.
2. **Several documented Wi-Fi and UI workflows are still scaffolding.**

The docs describe AP mode, station selection, NTP sync, cloud upload selection/progress, file viewing, and RTC setting: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:3), [wifi-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/wifi-workflow.md:9).
- selecting a configured station, AP, cloud upload, and NTP action only logs: [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:530)
That would avoid the docs reading like finished product behavior while these surfaces are still stubs.
3. **The call-recording UI behavior has drifted from the written UI contract.**

The docs say the user can change call volume, mute/unmute the mic, and see a 3-pixel tally border that becomes dotted when local mic is muted: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:7). The Bluetooth workflow also says the recording screen shows mute status and output volume: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:19).
- exposes push-to-talk mode switching and **speaker** mute in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:185)

- uses a fixed 10-pixel solid red frame in [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:21) and [RecordingView.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:403)
- has mic mute support in `AudioManager`, but I did not find GUI wiring for it: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1307)
- has volume APIs, but not a recording-view volume control: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1231)
Suggested change: choose one UI model.
- If the device is primarily push-to-talk on internal hardware, rewrite the docs around P2T and speaker mute.
- If mic mute and volume are still required call controls, add those controls and make the tally border encode mic state as documented.
4. **Recorder performance/file-format docs are slightly ahead of current behavior.**

The file-format doc says new recordings should pre-allocate roughly 1 GB and truncate afterward: [recording-file-format.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/recording-file-format.md:7). The dual-core note also assumes pre-grown `.rec` files: [dual-core-load-distribution.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/dual-core-load-distribution.md:113).

Current code has preallocation implemented but disabled by default: [conf.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:14), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:418).

Also, the doc says packets have a header and payload; the current on-disk implementation writes a **fixed-size** `file_packet_t` for normal `.rec` packets, including a fixed payload array and `payload_length`: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:31), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:230).
- update the format doc to explicitly say `.rec` packets are fixed-size today
- say preallocation is optional/compile-time unless you intend to enable it again
5. **The high-level audio/hardware docs overstate two areas.**

The high-level design says an external WM8960 or SGTL5000 codec may be enabled by build flag: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:9). In the current audio manager, external I2S codec enable functions are explicit “not implemented yet” stubs: [AudioManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:626).

The same doc says CVSD local PCM is 8 kHz: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:31). The implementation accepts 8 kHz HFP, but `AudioFifo` upsamples 8 kHz input into its 16 kHz internal representation, and recorder packets are labeled `*_16KHZ_MONO`: [AudioFifo.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFifo/AudioFifo.h:90), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:315).
- state “external codec path planned, internal M5Stack audio path implemented”
- HFP CVSD ingress/egress can be 8 kHz
- recorder/internal FIFO normalizes to 16 kHz today

- Bluetooth connection auto-starts a call recording after entering a connected/audio-available state, matching the Bluetooth workflow: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:13), [main.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:166).

- The recording stop path can also disconnect Bluetooth: [bluetooth-workflow.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/bluetooth-workflow.md:17), [RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp:64).

- Wi-Fi activation shuts down Bluetooth/audio/recording first, which matches the high-level design: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:17), [WifiManager.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/WifiManager/WifiManager.cpp:291).

- RTC-based recording naming and `.rec` output are in place: [high-level-fw-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/high-level-fw-design.md:21), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:68).
- Top bar date/time and battery status are implemented: [ui-design.md](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/developer-docs/ui-design.md:16), [FlyGui.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:630).

Ignoring the eventual removal of `bluetooth.json` and `wifi.json` as requested: the recording format already has security hooks, but they are placeholders right now. `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY` adds nonce fields and leaves encryption/authentication as TODOs: [defs.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:39), [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:206).
Before hardening transfer/config storage, I would write one short security design doc that states:
- how keys are provisioned and recovered
- what local UI actions are allowed without trust/authentication
- whether the HTTP file surface survives at all
I did not count `bluetooth.json` or `wifi.json` mismatches in the findings. I also did not run builds or tests for this pass; this was a documentation-to-code review.

user_prompt: the other weird part: I don't remember it being slow, did something change?
agent_reply: Yes. The likely change is from the microSD fatal-error work we just added.

Now [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:401) also calls `MicroSdCard::health()` **before every recording file open**. That new health check in [MicroSdCard.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/MicroSdCard.cpp:121) does:

That free-cluster scan is the prime suspect for a multi-second delay on SdFat, especially on a larger or fuller card. `readCID()` is a deliberate card-presence probe and should be comparatively small; the free-space calculation is the part I would not want on the hot path for “tap memo, start now”.

I checked the recent committed memo path too: `RecordingView::beginMemoRecording()` and `RecordingViewCallbacks::beginMemoRecording()` have not picked up a new slow step in the committed history. Also `ENABLE_FILE_PREALLOCATION` is currently commented out in [conf.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:14), so `grow_file()` is not the reason.
- On recording start, do only the cheap missing/unreadable probe before open.
- Only if opening fails, do the slower classification work to decide whether to report “card missing/unreadable”, “card full”, or generic file-open failure.
That still keeps microSD failures fatal and gives the better error when something goes wrong, without making every memo start pay for a free-space scan.

user_prompt: I want to run `void test_sdcard()` to test performance of the recorder with encryption enabled

agent_reply: Wired `test_sdcard()` so it should actually test encrypted recorder performance now.
- `setup()` now calls `all_init()` before bring-up tests, so NVS/M5/gui basics exist before `Aegis` and SD setup.
- installs a deterministic test master key only if no key already exists,
- forces `AudioFileRecorder::setPurePcmMode(false)` so it does not bypass encryption.
- `AudioFileRecorder` now includes `Aegis` by relative path so PlatformIO library discovery works.
- `BtHostList.h` and `WifiManager.h` now include `conf.h`, so their security-mode structs match their `.cpp` files.
Build passed. I restored the generated `src/version.c` bump afterward.

user_prompt: the encrypted block size might be too small, so the overhead is too large

agent_reply: Done. The encrypted plaintext block size is now configured in [inc/conf.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:51) as:
// 8192 bytes is approximately 128ms worth of 16 kHz 16-bit stereo PCM data.
I removed the hardcoded `1024` from [inc/defs.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/defs.h:17), and `defs.h` now includes `conf.h` directly so the derived encrypted chunk sizes stay self-contained.

agent_reply: All project code under `src`, `lib`, and `inc` now uses `DBG_LOG*`; the only remaining `ESP_LOG*` references are inside the optional ESP-IDF backend in [dbg_log.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/dbg_log.h:1). I added `DBG_LOG_USE_SERIAL`, which routes logs through `Serial.printf` / `Serial.vprintf` and formats them like:
I also wired `platformio.ini` like this in [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini:75):

`CORE_DEBUG_LEVEL` and `LOG_LOCAL_LEVEL` stay as ESP-style values because framework headers like M5Unified read them before our `DBG_LOG_*` constants exist. Your project logging is controlled by `DBG_LOG_LOCAL_LEVEL`.

I checked the installed ESP32 SDK config and, yes, your suspicion looks right: the prebuilt package has `CONFIG_LOG_MAXIMUM_LEVEL=1`, so ESP-IDF logging above error is effectively capped. The new serial backend bypasses that.
Only the existing ESP-FTP-Server deprecated API warning showed up.

user_prompt: Write code in `lib\PinCode\PinCode.cpp` and its associated header file
A new NVS partition will be used, with just one key for the single `pin_code_cfg_t` instance of data, which is read and held in memory.

- log bad attempt, increments `bad_attempts` and see if it exceeds `PIN_CODE_MAX_BAD_ATTEMPTS_ALLOWED`, in which case, revoke the pin, save to NVS
- log successful usage, resets `bad_attempts` , save to NVS

- set the pin to a string (customization), mark it as `active` and log the date `date_set` and reset the salt to 0 , save to NVS

user_prompt: see the highlighted chunk of code
come up with proper constants for the case of using MP3 compression with `liblame`
agent_reply: Rationale: at 16 kHz, LAME uses 576 PCM frames per MP3 frame, so a 2-frame chunk is 1152 stereo frames, or 4608 bytes of 16-bit stereo PCM. Output buffer follows LAME’s documented `7200 + 1.25 * nsamples` sizing rule.

I also made [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp) use the shared recorder audio constants, but kept the current encrypted WAV buffer fixed at 8192 bytes until the MP3 write path is actually wired in. That avoids accidentally breaking the existing `.rec` chunk format.

I also made [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp) use the shared recorder audio constants, but kept the current encrypted WAV buffer fixed at 8192 bytes until the MP3 write path is actually wired in. That avoids accidentally breaking the existing `.rec` chunk format.

agent_reply: Adjusted [inc/conf.h](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h) for the 16 kHz stereo, 64 kbps CBR MP3 configuration.
So one encrypted MP3 chunk should represent about `144 ms` of audio and store `1152 + 28` bytes. That puts 10 minutes around `4.9 MB` encrypted, ignoring any extra file/header/tag overhead.

user_prompt: and if I simply edit MP3_LAME_MP3_FRAMES_PER_CHUNK to be like... 8, does it break anything? I believe some of the buffers are set differently
agent_reply: It should not inherently break anything if the MP3 path uses the derived macros consistently.
So yes, the buffers are different on purpose:
`MP3_PCM_BYTES_PER_CHUNK` is the raw stereo PCM input chunk: `18432` bytes.
`MP3_CBR_BYTES_PER_CHUNK` is the expected compressed MP3 output at 64 kbps CBR: `2304` bytes.
`MP3_LAME_OUTPUT_BUFFER_SIZE` is the conservative temporary encoder output buffer: `12960` bytes.

The only thing I see in your current `conf.h` is that line comment saying `// 1152 for ...=4` would become wrong if you change it to `8`. The formulas themselves should scale correctly. Bigger chunk size means less AES-GCM overhead and fewer file writes, but also more latency and larger temporary buffers. 8 frames / 288 ms is still totally reasonable.

Bigger chunk size means less AES-GCM overhead and fewer file writes, but also more latency and larger temporary buffers. 8 frames / 288 ms is still totally reasonable.

user_prompt: the `wav` files are simply too big for this device to be practical
I want to implement MP3 compression and decompression
I have already imported the `arduino-liblame` library
The `wav` code should not disappear, and I’ve added the switch `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION` for firmware versions that include the feature

Each mp3 frame can be variable length, this is a problem. We just modified the library to default to CBR mode with 64 kbps as the setting. This will help us estimate file time duration later, constants are defined in `inc\conf.h` to help us with this
The documentation `developer-docs\high-level-fw-design.md` and `developer-docs\recording-file-format.md` have been edited to include the new file extensions
The file format is simpler, there’s no RIFF header to write. But when the file is created, create the `MP3EncoderLAME` object, and configure it, register the callback. Do not create it on the stack!
The function `bool pump_audio_chunk_locked(bool force)` has a separate code path for the MP3 mode build, don’t bother calling `write_samples`, call `MP3EncoderLAME::write`  instead

The `MP3EncoderLAME`  callback either writes directly into the file if unencrypted, or, if encryption is used, it stages the data into a buffer, when the buffer is full or a new write is going to overflow it, then encrypt the buffer contents and write it to the card. When the `write` function is called on the file, set a flag saying so, we’ll need to know when this happens when cleaning up.

When the recording is ending, if it is not an emergency, we already have FIFO draining routine, let that finish, and then keep pumping in silence until one more file write happens (check the flag I mentioned before). Then call flush, as usual and then close the file, also of course clean up the `MP3EncoderLAME` instance
Stop work up to about here, do not do anything to playback or download or python mechanisms. I will test by recording real data, or ask you to write a test bench.
agent_reply: Done. I added the MP3 recording path behind `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION` without touching playback/download/python code.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:795) now creates a heap `MP3EncoderLAME`, writes PCM through `MP3EncoderLAME::write`, uses `.mp3` / `.fly`, and handles encrypted MP3 staging/writes.
- Stop/close now drains FIFO, pumps silence until another MP3 file write is observed, flushes the encoder, writes any final encrypted chunk, then closes/cleans up.
- [MP3EncoderLAME.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/arduino-liblame/src/MP3EncoderLAME.h:238) now actually forces CBR with `vbr_off`, keeps 64 kbps, disables bit reservoir, and exposes `flush()`.
Note: `src/version.c` is still dirty in the worktree, but I did not modify it.

user_prompt: test_mp3encode: target duration=10000 ms left=440.0 Hz right=660.0 Hz sample_rate=16000 Hz
test_mp3encode: submitted 160000 stereo frames over 16034 ms, max submit lag=6047 ms
test_mp3encode: stop/flush elapsed=4971 ms, total active+close elapsed=21005 ms
test_mp3encode: whole-frame-derived duration=10152 ms from 282 whole MP3 frames, remainder=0 bytes
also the start of the test is painfully slow
agent_reply: Yep, that log was weird in two ways:
1. It was genuinely behind real time: `16034 ms` to submit `10000 ms` of audio means the pump/encode path was falling about 6 seconds behind.
2. The test itself was adding bogus load: it was doing `sinf()` twice per sample, so 320,000 trig calls inside the timed window. That was not a fair recorder test.
I changed it so the next run should be much cleaner:
- `test_mp3encode` now uses a lookup-table sine generator instead of per-sample trig.
- It prints startup timing for sine init, `AudioManager`, SD init, and recorder start.
- It prints write timing after stop/flush.
- LAME is configured for fastest embedded mode: `MP3_LAME_QUALITY = 9`.
- LAME tags/ID3 output and info logs are disabled.
- CBR quantization skips unused masking work when noise shaping is off.
- CBR scratch allocation is reused instead of malloc/free every frame.
Run it again. The next log should tell us whether the remaining lag is actually LAME encode cost, SD write stalls, or startup setup.

user_prompt: how many things need to change if I change the MP3 encoder to 96 kbps CBR bit rate?
agent_reply: Everything important is derived from that: bytes/sec, MP3 frame bytes, encrypted chunk sizing, duration estimates in tests, and recorder configuration.
- At 96 kbps / 16 kHz, CBR MP3 frames are **432 bytes**.
- Not required for recording, because `AudioFileRecorder` explicitly sets it from `MP3_LAME_BITRATE_KBPS`, but it prevents future confusion.

No playback/download/python changes should be needed. The main side effect is files get **50% larger** than 64 kbps. The encrypted MP3 buffer math should still be fine: 1728 bytes per plaintext chunk is still comfortably under the older WAV chunk buffer size.

user_prompt: #define MP3_LAME_QUALITY                     9  // 0=best/slowest, 9=fastest; embedded recorder needs real-time encode
agent_reply: Filled it in in [inc/conf.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:75). For 64, 80, and 96 kbps the PCM frame count stays `576`; bitrate only changes the encoded MP3 frame byte size.
Also cleaned up the stale 64 kbps comment and removed the hardcoded `1152` comment from `MP3_CBR_BYTES_PER_CHUNK`.
Build passed. At 96 kbps, the derived values now come out to 432 bytes per MP3 frame, 1728 bytes per 4-frame chunk, and still 144 ms per chunk.

commit_message: significant work trying to get MP3 encoding to work

user_prompt: Excuse the interruption, your thoughts pointed out a potential friction point I want to resolve in a particular way, about the samples-per-pass

For context, in another session, we have started to add MP3 compression, to reduce file sizes and speed up download speeds. But the encoder `arduino-liblame` is a bit too slow, and we have some FIFO overflows during recording, the recordings are not completely unusable, but we are getting around 160 FIFO overflows in a 30 second recording while using bluetooth. So I want to explorer using a fixed point encoder to improve the performance just slightly, instead of more drastic actions like using mono instead of stereo. I have put `shine` encoder in the project to accomplish this. Read `.codex_history\lib\AudioFileRecorder\AudioFileRecorder.cpp.codexhist.json` for more context about what was recently changed and why.
It is to be a class object, very similar to `MP3EncoderLAME`
The constructor will take in parameters:
- callback with shape `typedef void (*MP3CallbackFDK)(uint8_t *mp3_data, size_t len);`
And then instantiate a copy of `shine_config_t` with those settings internally and store it
A `begin()` function will call `shine_initialise`, which will allocate and return a `shine_global_config`, and we store it
When `begin()` is called, get the data for `shine_samples_per_pass` and allocate internal buffers accordingly

A function `int32_t write(void *pcm_samples, int bytes)` , which uses the internal buffers, if we have buffered enough data, internally call `shine_encode_buffer_interleaved` and then if data is returned, call the callback function with data
Skip the buffering if the code realizes that the input size matches the input buffer size (and the buffer is empty). The point is to avoid just one more for-loop.
A function `flush()` that calls `shine_flush` and then immediately call the callback function with data
An `end()` function, clean up any allocated buffers, calls `shine_close`, and also, the destructor calls `end()` if it hasn't been called
Add a `library.json` file that sets up the compilation of this `ShineWrapper` library correctly, the `lib\ShineWrapper\shine\src\bin` is to be completely ignored
There is zero dependency on `arduino-liblame` or any of its contents

For all the stuff in `AudioFileRecorder` that uses `arduino-liblame`, put it all inside blocks activated by `BUILD_MP3_USE_LIBLAME` , and then using `BUILD_MP3_USE_SHINE`, implement the same operations using `ShineWrapper`.

You will need to think about if extra steps are needed to change FIFO dequeuing sizes, as I think by internally buffering again, we might not need to, but maybe there’s performance gains if we actually did not internally buffer.

When the file is finished recording, the code path for `liblame` is a bit complicated as I didn’t understand how to safely flush, but `shine` has a `flush` so use it instead of stuffing zeros until it works
There might be more constants needed in `inc/conf.h` or maybe some of them with `_LAME_` inside might get renamed to be more generic.
agent_reply: - Split `AudioFileRecorder` MP3 encoder code between `BUILD_MP3_USE_LIBLAME` and `BUILD_MP3_USE_SHINE`.
- Shine now uses `getSamplesPerPass()` after `begin()`, and normal recorder pumping waits for a full encoder pass so the wrapper usually takes the no-copy fast path.
- Shine stop/close uses `flush()` directly; the LAME silence-padding workaround is now LAME-only.
- Renamed MP3 constants in [conf.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h) to generic names and removed the always-on arduino-liblame include paths/dependency.
Note: the worktree already has unrelated dirty Shine bin files and `src/version.c`; I left those alone.
- Split `AudioFileRecorder` MP3 encoder code between `BUILD_MP3_USE_LIBLAME` and `BUILD_MP3_USE_SHINE`.
- Shine now uses `getSamplesPerPass()` after `begin()`, and normal recorder pumping waits for a full encoder pass so the wrapper usually takes the no-copy fast path.
- Shine stop/close uses `flush()` directly; the LAME silence-padding workaround is now LAME-only.
- Renamed MP3 constants in [conf.h](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h) to generic names and removed the always-on arduino-liblame include paths/dependency.

commit_message: small reorg of conf.h and defs.h

user_prompt: the diagnostic mechanism is telling me that during a bluetooth call session with recording active, if my local mic is off, then the redraw rate is 60 FPS as expected as it is capped at 60, but when I use push-to-talk, the frame rate drops to 45 FPS
the diagnostics show that 16000 samples goes both ways in both circumstances, and I don't detect any FIFO overflows, and everything does seem to function correctly

but the frame rate dropping is weird, the audio processing, the AGC and HPF, those are all core0, while the GUI draws are core1. The file writing and encoding are done after 2 mono streams are mixed into stereo so it shouldn't matter if the mic is on or off
agent_reply: You’re probably missing that the recorder/encoder is on core1 with the GUI, not core0.

In [main.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:258), `gui->poll()` runs on core1, then [AudioFileRecorder::pump()](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:262) runs immediately after it on the same core. The recording view sets audio mode to a 60 Hz GUI poll cap in [RecordingView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:163), and FPS is counted only when `gui->poll()` actually draws in [FlyGui.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:163). So if the recorder pump gets heavier, the GUI simply misses 60 Hz slots.

The key detail: MP3 compression is enabled in [conf.h](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:21). With mic off, the mic channel going to the recording is synthesized silence via `queue_silence_to_match()` in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:913). With push-to-talk, the mic channel is real signal and gets mixed into stereo, then encoded by `write_mp3_samples()` in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1771). Same 16k samples/sec, different encoder cost. Silence is cheap; live mic noise/speech is not.

There’s also a mode-routing change: PTT enters mic mode and chokes the speaker FIFO in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:856), so the recorder is pairing real mic FIFO data with BT host FIFO data rather than pairing BT host data with generated silence. That can change burstiness and mutex/FIFO pressure even when average sample rates look perfect.

I’d verify by timing `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` total duration and separately timing `write_mp3_samples()`. Your existing long-write stats only catch SD/encrypted write duration, not encoder time. Quick A/B: temporarily disable `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION`; if PTT goes back to 60 FPS, that’s the smoking crater.

That can change burstiness and mutex/FIFO pressure even when average sample rates look perfect.

I’d verify by timing `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` total duration and separately timing `write_mp3_samples()`. Your existing long-write stats only catch SD/encrypted write duration, not encoder time. Quick A/B: temporarily disable `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION`; if PTT goes back to 60 FPS, that’s the smoking crater.
